coredata.dc:title: Red emissive nanocomposite with high quantum yield for ultrasensitive and selective detection of latent fingerprints
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Qianqian
Wang, Wen
Sun, Guangting
Albert Aryee, Aaron
Wei, Jinbao
Meng, Hong-min
Kong, Weigang
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Level 1–3 minutiae of latent fingerprints (LFPs) play essential roles in the identification of individuals. The level 3 details of sweat pores are especially important for accurate LFPs recognition, as they can provide microscopic features. Fluorescent materials with high quantum yield and red emission are advantageous in the LFPs detection, due to the associated potential of elevated sensitivity and contrast. Herein, a highly fluorescent red emissive nanocomposite material (PPDCDs@ZIF-8) was successfully prepared by combining carbonized polymer dots of PPDCDs with zeolitic imidazolate framework ZIF-8 in situ. The obtained material displays a strong and stable red emission at solid state with the maximum peak located at 630 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield of PPDCDs is significantly improved from 17.8% to 43.5% after conjugation with ZIF-8. This nanocomposite material was successfully applied to detect LFPs on glass, metal, marble and other substrates with ultrahigh sensitivity. The obtained fluorescent images clearly revealed the detailed LFPs features from level 1 to level 3. Therefore, PPDCDs@ZIF-8 possesses great potential applications in crime scenes as it provides an easy and accurate strategy to visual the sweat pores in LFPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state fluorescence
Carbonized polymer dots
Zeolitic imidazolate framework
Latent fingerprint
Sweat pore

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure characterizations: TEM image of PPDCDs (a), and size distribution (b), SEM images of ZIF-8 (c) and PPDCDs@ZIF-8 (d), and TEM images of ZIF-8 (e), PPDCDs@ZIF-8 (f, g), and PXRD patterns of ZIF-8 PPDCDs@ZIF-8 and simulated spectrum from crystallographic data of ZIF-8 (h).

The effect of PPDCDs content on the fluorescence property of PPDCDs@ZIF-8: emission spectra (a), the plot of the maximum emission intensity versus the PPDCDs content (b), the corresponding color plot (c), the relationship between fluorescence quantum yields and PPDCDs content (d), time-resolved fluorescence spectra (e), the plot of calculated lifetime values versus PPDCDs content (f).

Latent fingerprints images taken in the bright field (a-j) and dark field (a1-j1) under 365 nm UV beam, respectively. The substrates from front to back are culture disk, marble, aluminum foil, coin, paper money, glass slide, black plastic cover, painted wood, stainless steel mold, and weighing paper, respectively.

Fluorescent images of LFPs on the aluminum foil with the corresponding magnification of level 1–3 features in a-e: bifurcation (a), island and bifurcation (b), combination (c), loop, bridge and bifurcation (d), and sweat pores (e).
Schematic illustration of the synthetic route of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 and LFPs detection with level 3 details of sweat pores.

head.ce:title: Red emissive nanocomposite with high quantum yield for ultrasensitive and selective detection of latent fingerprints

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Evidence Appraisal Institute, Zhengzhou Public Security Bureau, Zhengzhou 450000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Evidence Appraisal Institute, Zhengzhou Public Security Bureau, Zhengzhou 450000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly fluorescent red emissive nanocomposite (PPDCDs@ZIF-8) was successfully prepared by combining carbonized polymer dots of PPDCDs with the metal organic framework of ZIF-8. The obtained material displays a strong and stable red emission at solid state, with a greatly improved fluorescence quantum yield. This nanocomposite material was successfully applied to detect latent fingerprints (LFPs) with the detailed features from level 1 to level 3.

Level 1–3 minutiae of latent fingerprints (LFPs) play essential roles in the identification of individuals. The level 3 details of sweat pores are especially important for accurate LFPs recognition, as they can provide microscopic features. Fluorescent materials with high quantum yield and red emission are advantageous in the LFPs detection, due to the associated potential of elevated sensitivity and contrast. Herein, a highly fluorescent red emissive nanocomposite material (PPDCDs@ZIF-8) was successfully prepared by combining carbonized polymer dots of PPDCDs with zeolitic imidazolate framework ZIF-8 in situ. The obtained material displays a strong and stable red emission at solid state with the maximum peak located at 630 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield of PPDCDs is significantly improved from 17.8% to 43.5% after conjugation with ZIF-8. This nanocomposite material was successfully applied to detect LFPs on glass, metal, marble and other substrates with ultrahigh sensitivity. The obtained fluorescent images clearly revealed the detailed LFPs features from level 1 to level 3. Therefore, PPDCDs@ZIF-8 possesses great potential applications in crime scenes as it provides an easy and accurate strategy to visual the sweat pores in LFPs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprints have played a vital role in forensic investigations for many decades, due to their uniqueness to individuals, which aid in individual identification [1–3]. For most of the crime scenes, the fingerprints are invisible to the naked eyes, thus are called as latent fingerprints (LFPs). Minutiae of LFPs, which are typically divided into three levels, are crucial for individual recognition [4–6]. Level 1 describes the overall characteristic of fingerprints, which is not sufficient for identification. Level 2 refers to the discriminative macroscopic features present in papillary ridges, for example, ridge bifurcation, combination, short ridge, and so on. Level 3 is related to the microscopic characteristics, including sweat pores, ridge edge shape and ridge width. Level 3 features, especially sweat pores, have attracted intense interest in the detection of LFPs, as they can provide quantitative data for accurate LFPs recognition. Hence, it is crucial to develop an effective strategy for the detection of LFPs with level 3 details of sweat pores.

To date, diverse methods have been developed for the visual detection of LFPs [5,7–10]. Among them, utilizing photoluminescent (PL) materials is regarded as a prominent approach for fast and effective detection of LFPs. LFPs on various types of substrates have been reported to be visualized with minutiae features [11–13]. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have emerged as an attractive class of nanomaterials, owing to their tunable PL properties, excellent resistance to photobleaching, good biocompatibility, and nontoxicity [14–19]. CPDs show great potential in their application for LFPs visualization. A green emissive CPDs solution, prepared with DL-malic acid and ethylenediamine, was reported to detect LFPs under pH 9 [20]. Orange emitting CPDs were reported for fast detection of LFPs by immersing in the CPDs solution, and second level features could be visualized under UV light [21]. The small size of the uniformly distributed sphere-like CPDs nanoparticles, as well as the enriched functional groups on the surface, is beneficial for the LFPs detection, owing to favorable adhesion of CPDs particles on to the ridges of LFPs. Despite these merits, there remains several obstacles that limit the widespread application of these materials in LFPs detection, especially at crime scenes. From the limited reports on the application of CPDs, it can be observed that CPDs were mainly used as uniformly dispersed suspensions, in consequence of PL quenching at solid or aggregated state. However, the solution method is commonly considered to be inconvenient for wide applications at real crime scenes, due to the following drawbacks: (1) most of the CPDs solutions applied in LFPs detection are blue- or green-emissive [20,22], making it hard to shield from the background interference and resulting in poor contrast of LFPs detection. (2) The solution treatment process may destroy or obviously eliminate biomarkers in LFPs residues, making it difficult for the further detection of biomarkers, which is usually carried out after LFPs development in forensic investigations. (3) The operations are usually complicated and require a relatively long incubation time. Besides, the obtained details of LFPs are usually in level 2, and thus the detecting sensitivity is very low. Therefore, it is essential to develop a red-emissive CPDs-based solid material to address the abovementioned problems.

To address the afore-mentioned issues, a strong red emissive CPDs, named PPDCDs, was synthesized with 4-piperidinoaniline (PPD) as a precursor. As reported, metal–organic frameworks are suitable to stabilize the emission of carbon-based nanomaterials at solid state, due to the prevention of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching [11,23]. In this work, zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) was employed to stabilize the red emission of PPDCDs at solid state. The resultant composite material PPDCDs@ZIF-8 successfully provided excellent solid-state red luminescence. Not only the emission of PPDCDs was effectively preserved, but also the emission was greatly enhanced in solid PPDCDs@ZIF-8. The absolute quantum yield of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 was found to be 43.5% which was an enhancement of that for PPDCDs (17.8%). The resulting red emissive powder was successfully applied for LFPs detection with enhanced contrast and high sensitivity on representative substrates, such as glass, plastic, metal, painted wood, marble and so on. The developed LFPs were clearly visualized with level 3 details of sweat pores. The synthetic route of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 and the process of LFPs detection are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

4-piperidinoaniline (PPD) was purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). 2-methylimidazole (99%) and zinc nitrate hexahydrate (99%) were obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. All regents were analytical grade and used without further purification.

The size distribution was measured by a NanoPlus3 Zeta potential and nano particle size analyzer. The bulk phase purities of ZIF-8 and the corresponding PPDCDs@ZIF-8 were verified by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54056 Å) in the range of 5–60°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were performed on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet IS 50 FTIR spectrometer. The solid UV–vis reflectance spectra of the samples were measured on an Agilent Cary 5000 UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere attachment. All the fluorescence properties of the samples were recorded using an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer. The surface chemistry of the synthesized PPDCDs, ZIF-8 and PPDCDs@ZIF-8 was examined using a Kratos AXIS Supra + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on a Tecnai G2 F20S electron microscope. TEM specimens were prepared by depositing an appropriate amount of the corresponding dispersion onto the sample holder, and subsequently drying naturally before being measured.

PPDCDs were synthesized through a solvothermal method at 160 °C for 12 h, according to a previous work [14]. In brief, 50 mg of 4-piperidinoaniline (PPD) was added into 5 mL of absolute ethanol, and then sonicated in order to get a uniformly dispersed solution. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for solvothermal reaction at 160 °C for 12 h. After that, the crude products were purified by silica column chromatography to obtain the desired PPDCDs.

The composite PPDCDs@ZIF-8 was synthesized through a one-step self-assembly method under ambient conditions [24–26]. The detailed synthesis is described as below. Firstly, 0.9918 g (3.0 mmol) of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 25 mL of methanol. Separately, 1.0267 g (12.0 mmol) of 2-methylimidazole (Mim) was completely dissolved in 25 mL of methanol and a desired amount of PPDCDs solution was added into the above Mim solution. Zinc solution was then added into the Mim solution under sonication at room temperature. The pink PPDCDs@ZIF-8 product was separated by centrifuging and the product was washed with methanol for three times. The yield was 89% based on the starting Zn(NO3)2·6H2O.

LFPs on different substrates were collected from the index and middle finger of volunteers. Before LFPs were deposited on the selected substrates, the volunteers were required to wash their hands with running water and dry their hands naturally. Then, the fingers gently touched the area of forehead and nose before LFPs were left. The deposited LFPs were detected with PPDCDs@ZIF-8 by powder-dusting method, and excess material was carefully removed under inert gas flow.

The morphology of the prepared materials was characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the pristine PPDCDs display a monodispersed, spherical shape [27–29]. The particle size varies in the range of 0.8–3.2 nm, with the mean size of 2.0 nm (Fig. 1b). SEM images of ZIF-8 and PPDCDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 1c, d) both display a regular hexagonal prism structure, with an average particle size of 100–200 nm. The TEM images of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 (Fig. 1e, f) also show clear hexagonal shapes of the particles. Despite this, lattice fringes are revealed on the edge of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 in the TEM image (Fig. 1g). The interplanar distance is measured to be 0.21 nm, indicating the formation of graphene carbon. PXRD was performed to confirm if the framework of ZIF-8 was preserved in PPDCDs@ZIF-8, after the combination of PPDCDs. As delineated in Fig. 1h, diffraction peaks of the pristine ZIF-8 and the resultant PPDCDs@ZIF-8 are very sharp and strong, indicating high crystallinity in them. The measured PXRD patterns are identical to each other and consistent with the simulated one, derived from the crystallographic data of ZIF-8 [30]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 appear at 7.3°, 10.3°, 12.7°, 14.7°, 16.4°, and 18.0° corresponding to the (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), and (222) planes, respectively, in the ZIF-8 structure. The result indicates the same framework of the resulting PPDCDs@ZIF-8 as the raw ZIF-8, after the encapsulation of PPDCDs.

Chemical composition of ZIF-8 and the resulting PPDCDs@ZIF-8 were further investigated by Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping. The mapping results presented in Fig. S1 demonstrate the existence of Zn/C/N in ZIF-8 and Zn/C/N/O in PPDCDs@ZIF-8, respectively. Compared with ZIF-8, the appearance of oxygen atoms in PPDCDs@ZIF-8 is attributed to the introduction of PPDCDs into ZIF-8 framework.

The chemical composition and valence states of these materials were also explored by XPS measurement. The wide scan survey spectrum (Fig. S2) indicates the coexistence of Zn, C, N, and O in PPDCDs@ZIF-8, consistent with the EDS results. The elemental analysis results of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 are calculated to be C 53.6%, O 6.2%, N 14.0%, and Zn 26.2%. The high resolution of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Zn 2p spectra were also examined. Deconvolution of XPS spectra was performed to determine the chemical state of each element in PPDCDs@ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. S3a, the devolution of C ls spectrum exhibited three peaks corresponding to C=C/C–C (284.7 eV, 54.33%), C–N/C–O (285.5 eV, 43.54%) and C=O (288.4 eV, 2.13%). The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3b) was deconvoluted into three peaks which were assigned to amine N (398.7 eV), N from Mim ligands (399.0 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) [31]. The relative ratios of the above N species are 8.52%, 63.46%, and 28.02%, respectively. The two peaks in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3c) are attributed to the C=O (531.9 eV) and C–O/O–H (532.4 eV). The peaks at 1021.9 eV and 1044.9 eV (Fig. S3d) are assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively, suggesting the presence of Zn element in the form of Zn2+, which are in good accordance with those reported in literature [32,33]. XPS measurements, as well as EDS mapping, successfully confirm the self-assembly of PPDCDs into ZIF-8 matrix.

The chemical functional groups of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 were further explored by assessing the corresponding FTIR spectra as illustrated in Fig. S4. For PPDCDs, the broad and strong absorption band at 3436 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O−H/N−H, whereas the absorption at 2936 and 2855 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of C−H bonds. The absorption band at 1640 cm−1 originates from C=O/C=N with the observed small broad band at 1440 cm−1 attributed to the C=C bonds of the benzene ring [34,35]. The peaks located at 1129/1026 cm−1 are assigned to C−O and C−N bonds, respectively. With regards to PPDCDs@ZIF-8, the corresponding IR spectrum not only contains the characteristic absorption derived from PPDCDs, but also contains the characteristic absorptions attributed to the framework of ZIF-8. Compared with that of PPDCDs, the new peaks in PPDCDs@ZIF-8 originates from Mim of ZIF-8. The intense absorption bands in the range of 1350–1500 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of imidazole ring. The absorption at 1177 cm−1 is related to C–N vibration. The adsorption peaks in 600–1000 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibration of the imidazole ring. The sharp peak at 422 cm−1 is ascribed to Zn–N stretching mode, consistent with the previously reported ZIF-8 [36].

Optical performance of the prepared composite material was investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As presented in Fig. S5, the pristine ZIF-8 has negligible absorption in the range of 250–800 nm, whereas the PPDCDs methanol solution shows a significant absorption centered at ∼ 300 nm and a very weak broad absorption at ∼ 500 nm. After the incorporation of a tiny amount of PPDCDs into ZIF-8 matrix, a relatively intense absorption band appears at 500 nm, accompanied with small peaks at about 270 and 320 nm. This indicates that the resulting composite material displays an enhanced light absorption ability, (which mainly originates from PPDCDs), with a potential for an enhanced PL emission.

As shown in Fig. S6, the PPDCDs solution displays a red emission under 365 nm irradiation. When a small amount of PPDCDs was introduced in the synthesis of composite materials, the resulting materials exhibit a light pink color under day light and a bright red luminescence under 365 nm UV light. Even the white tablet pressed with a very tiny amount of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 and KBr (weight ratio of 1:120), could emit an obvious red light upon UV irradiation.

The corresponding fluorescence properties were measured to explore the influence of the added amounts of PPDCDs on the fluorescence performances of the composite materials. As illustrated in Fig. 2
a, the emission spectra are very similar for different PPDCDs-containing materials. They all present intense emission peaks centered at about 630 nm and small weak emissions at 450 nm. The fluorescence intensities at 450 nm are identical for different PPDCDs@ZIF-8 materials, ascribed to the emission from the ZIF-8 matrix. With the increasing of PPDCDs content, fluorescence intensities at 630 nm reveal a gradual increasing trend (Fig. 2b). The maximum intensity was obtained when 30 µL of PPDCDs was added. The corresponding color coordinates (Fig. 2c) are shifted with the content of PPDCDs, from (0.50, 0.35) to (0.63, 0.35). The color coordinates are (0.63, 0.35) for the addition of 30 µL PPDCDs, which are closer to the coordinates of standard red (0.67, 0.33).

The absolute quantum yield of the starting PPDCDs solution is measured to be 17.8%. Upon the introduction of PPDCDs nanoparticles into the ZIF-8 framework, the quantum yields of the obtained composite materials are greatly enhanced which can be ascribed to the elimination of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 2d, the quantum yield is dramatically increased from 17.8% to 43.5%, when 30 µL of PPDCDs was added. Fluorescence decays were further recorded to explore the influence of PPDCDs content on fluorescent properties of the resulting composite materials (Fig. 2e). The obtained lifetime values (Fig. 2f) are all distributed in nanosecond range, similarly to the starting PPDCDs solution. The values display a slowly reduced change with an increase in PPDCDs content. Considering the importance of fluorescence intensity and quantum yields in the detection of LFPs, the addition of 30 µL PPDCDs was selected as the optimum synthetic condition for the composite material.

The emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths were also measured at the optimum synthetic conditions, and the results are plotted in Fig. S7. According to the figure, the emission of the prepared PPDCDs@ZIF-8 is independent of the excitation wavelength, an indication of only one predominant emissive site with the maximum transition probability in the material [14,37–39]. The corresponding color coordinates were plotted in Fig. S8. The points are all distributed in the range of red color, with the values varying from (0.42, 0.26) to (0.71, 0.30). Besides, photostability is an important performance metric to be kept in mind, when developing fluorescent materials for LFPs detection. The emission stability of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. S9) shows that the resulted emission intensity does not change significantly under continuous illumination, demonstrating that the PPDCDs@ZIF-8 possesses excellent stability against photobleaching. The afore-mentioned results confirm that combining PPDCDs with the popular ZIF-8 matrix allows the resultant PPDCDs@ZIF-8 to exhibit a stable red emission with an enhanced quantum yield at solid state, which is favorable for the following application in LFPs detection.

Based on its high quantum yield at solid state, the red emissive powder of PPDCDs@ZIF-8 was then applied to detect LFPs on different substrates. The selected substrates are the representative types, which are commonly found at real crime scenes. As the Fig. 3 presented, the LFPs deposited on the substrates are very blurry and hard to be visualized by naked eyes under day light. When excited by 365 nm UV lamp in the dark, the blurry LFPs could be easily visualized. From the captured photographs obtained by a commercial smartphone, LFPs features are clearly observed with high contrast and low background interference, especially on glass, plastic, marble, coin, and aluminum foil. Compared with the photographs taken under daylight, those taken under UV light clearly display the flow of red-colored ridges and colorless furrows on the glossy glass slide, colored painted-wood, low contrast marble, fluorescent plastic culture dish, highly reflective aluminum foil or patterned coins.

To verify the fingerprint details, minutiae of LFPs on the marble and reflective aluminum foil were respectively analyzed. The images in Fig. 4 a-d display the level 1 and 2 features, from a to d: bifurcation; island & bifurcation; combination; loop & bridge & bifurcation. Apart from these minutiae, abundant sweat pores (circled in red) can be distinguished in the magnified ridges (Fig. 4e). The sweat pores are also clearly visualized in LFPs on the marble in the Fig. S10. Various shapes of sweat pores are presented in the magnified ridges. The shapes are presented as round, oval, angular, or even irregular shape. The opening of some of the sweat pores is larger or even cut off the ridges, while some of them are very small and difficult to be found. These well-defined sweat pores are helpful in the individual identification, especially for complicated LFPs or incomplete LFPs.

The LFPs detected on the marble were used for the assessment of the developing contrast. Both the fluorescent LFPs image and the corresponding grayscale image are provided in Fig. S7a, b. The contrast of LFPs was evaluated by the fluctuation of emission intensity between the ridges and furrows, plotted in Fig. S7c. The intensity is sharply fluctuated between the ridges and furrows. Secretions are distributed on the ridges of fingers, thereby transferring to the substrates when contacting the surface with fingers and forming LFPs. Whereas for the furrows, no secretions are present. In the detection of LFPs, the fluorescent powder will adhere to secretions of the papillary ridges, thus resulting in a high emission intensity. While for the furrows, the composite material hardly adheres to them and thus no emission will happen. The corresponding emission fluctuation across the red line in Fig. S7b shows that the intensity is strongly varied between the papillary ridges and the furrows, indicating a high developing contrast for the LFPs detection with the synthesized PPDCDs@ZIF-8. Compared with other fluorescent materials [ref], the proposed LFPs detection by red emissive PPDCDs@ZIF-8 exhibits an ultrahigh sensitivity and contrast. The results are listed in Table S1.

The selected substrates in this work covers diverse types of surfaces, typically found at the real crime scenes. These included glass slide, plastics, aluminum foil, highly fluorescent culture dish, anti-adhesive weighing paper, patterned coins, painted wood, and marble. The inherence of substrates varies from smooth to rough, from non-fluorescent to florescent, from permeable to impermeable. No matter what kind of substrates the LFPs are left on, the detecting results are desirable. Red emissive LFPs with level 2 or even level 3 features are visualized. The PPDCDs@ZIF-8 powder with a higher quantum yield has a more efficiency of converting absorbed light into red fluorescence and emits a striking fluorescence at an extremely low usage. This solid fluorescent material not only enables the LFPs detection to exhibit high contrast and sensitivity, but also makes the detection to be completed in no more than 5 min. Thereby, the PPDCDs@ZIF-8 material is very suitable and convenient for onsite LFPs detection in real crime scenes.

In conclusion, a red-emissive LFPs material PPDCDs@ZIF-8 was synthesized through a one-step self-assembly process under ambient conditions. With a tiny amount of PPDCDs introduced in the ZIF-8 matrix, an enhanced and stable red fluorescence is observed at solid state, avoiding aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence properties of the composite PPDCDs@ZIF-8 were measured elaborately. The corresponding fluorescence quantum yields and emission intensity are dramatically enhanced, in comparison with PPDCDs solution. By virtue of the strong and stable red emissive property at solid state, PPDCDs@ZIF-8 was implemented in the visual detection of LFPs on representative substrates. The fluorescent LFPs images on a variety of substrates provided evident LFPs details from level 1 to level 3. Sweat pores were clearly visualized in the LFPs ridges. The whole detection was finished in no more than 5 min. This work provides a convenient, onsite, and fast method for LFPs detection in crime scenes. The highly emissive nanocomposite is beneficial for ultrahigh sensitivity and improved contrast of LFPs detection. It is promising that this material could be applied in forensic science and related fluorescent imaging fields.

Qianqian Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Wen Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Guangting Sun: Investigation, Formal analysis. Aaron Albert Aryee: Writing – review & editing. Jinbao Wei: Investigation, Formal analysis. Hong-min Meng: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Weigang Kong: Formal analysis. Zhaohui Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Humanities and Social Sciences Youth Foundation of Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China (21YJC820056), and the National College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202210459153).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108688.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in background-free latent fingerprint imaging
Nano Res.

Anal. Methods

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent nanomaterials for the development of latent fingerprints in forensic sciences
Adv. Funct. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Wang, M. Li, A. Yu, Y. Zhu, M. Yang, C. Mao, Fluorescent nanomaterials for the development of latent fingerprints in forensic sciences, Adv. Funct. Mater., 27 (2017) 1606243.
